_work.title: Further Famous Alchemical Emblem-Books (1550–1752)
_work.creator: various
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The famous alchemical emblem-books and laboratory plates beyond the canonical Atalanta Fugiens, Splendor Solis and Rosarium: Michael Maier's Septimana Philosophica and Viatorium, the Pandora (Reusner, 1582), the Pretiosissimum Donum Dei flask-sequence, the Aureum Vellus of Trismosin, the Musaeum Hermeticum (1678) frontispiece, the Hermaphroditisches Sonn- und Mondskind (1752), Barchusen's process-emblems, and the great laboratory engravings of Libavius (the Domus Chymici) and Glauber (the Furni Novi Philosophici).

entries.title: *Septimana Philosophica* (1620) — Title Engraving
entries.creator: Michael Maier
entries.summary:
The engraved title-page of Michael Maier's *Septimana Philosophica* (Frankfurt, 1620), in which Solomon, the Queen of Sheba and Hiram of Tyre exchange the riddles of nature over a philosophical week.

## Iconography
The frame gathers the three royal interlocutors named in the long Latin title — Solomon, the wisest of the Israelites, the Arabian Queen of Sheba, and Hiram, prince of Tyre — around the emblematic apparatus of the dialogue, with mottoes keyed to the seven-day structure of the book.

## Significance
The *Septimana Philosophica* casts the whole of [[motif:alchemy|natural philosophy]] as a courtly contest of *aenigmata* (riddles), one of the last works of Maier and a companion to his *Atalanta Fugiens*. The Solomon-and-Sheba framing ties the alchemical quest to biblical wisdom literature, a favourite strategy of the German adepts for claiming sacred authority.

## For artists & game designers
A riddle-tournament framing device: three crowned sages trading natural puzzles across seven days — a ready quest structure.

entries.title: *Septimana Philosophica* — Emblematic Plate (Wellcome)
entries.creator: Michael Maier
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A plate from Michael Maier's *Septimana Philosophica* (1620), preserved in the Wellcome Collection's crisp scan.

## Iconography
The engraving belongs to the emblematic apparatus of Maier's seven-day philosophical dialogue, pairing figurative scene and motto in the manner of his other emblem-books.

## Significance
The *Septimana Philosophica* extended Maier's project of teaching [[motif:alchemy|the art]] through emblem and *aenigma*. The Wellcome plate witnesses the visual programme of one of his lesser-known but historically important works, issued in the same Frankfurt milieu that produced the *Atalanta Fugiens* and the *Musaeum Hermeticum*.

## For artists & game designers
A spare motto-and-figure card useful as a 'lore fragment' drop in an alchemy-themed deck.

citations.text: Michael Maier, *Septimana Philosophica* (Frankfurt, 1620); Wellcome Collection L0028882.

entries.title: *Viatorium* (1618) — De Montibus Planetarum Septem
entries.creator: Michael Maier
entries.summary:
A page from Michael Maier's *Viatorium, hoc est, De montibus planetarum septem seu metallorum* (1618), the "wayfaring book" of the seven planetary mountains.

## Iconography
The engraving belongs to Maier's allegory of the seven metals as mountains, each governed by its planet, which the philosophical traveller must ascend in turn. Emblematic scenes and Latin captions guide the reader along the route.

## Significance
The [[motif:viatorium|Viatorium]] frames the [[motif:alchemy|opus]] as a journey through the seven [[motif:planetary metals|planetary metals]] — Saturn/lead, Jupiter/tin, Mars/iron, Venus/copper, Mercury/quicksilver, Luna/silver, Sol/gold. Maier's mountain-and-pilgrim conceit gives the doctrine of metal-planet correspondence a narrative, spatial form that anticipates later 'mountain of the adepts' imagery.

## For artists & game designers
Seven planetary mountains to ascend in sequence — a complete level-map: one metal-boss per peak.

citations.text: Michael Maier, *Viatorium, hoc est, De montibus planetarum septem* (1618).

entries.title: The Sixth Key (*Sixième Clef*) of Basil Valentine
entries.creator: Michael Maier, after Basilius Valentinus
entries.summary:
The Sixth Key of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, in the engraved version transmitted by Michael Maier — the emblem of the alchemical marriage.

## Iconography
A crowned king and queen are joined in a nuptial scene, attended by sun and moon and the emblematic apparatus of the union. The sixth key marks the *coniunctio* proper, the wedding of the solar and lunar principles within the vessel.

## Significance
The [[motif:twelve keys|Twelve Keys]] sequence steps the [[motif:philosophers stone|opus]] from purification to perfection; the sixth, the [[motif:coniunctio|conjunction]], is its hinge, the chemical wedding from which the [[motif:rebis hermaphrodite|rebis]] will be born. Maier helped fix the visual canon of the keys for the seventeenth century, and this plate became a standard image of the alchemical marriage.

## For artists & game designers
The 'fusion ceremony' beat: two royal sprites wed under sun and moon to unlock the combined form.

citations.text: Basilius Valentinus, *The Twelve Keys*; engraving after Michael Maier (1618).

entries.title: *Pandora* (1582) — Emblematic Plate (p. 165)
entries.creator: Hieronymus Reusner (compiler)
entries.summary:
An emblematic figure from the *Pandora, das ist, Die edleste Gab Gottes* ("Pandora, that is, the Noblest Gift of God"), compiled by Hieronymus Reusner and printed at Basel in 1582.

## Iconography
The plate belongs to the famous *Pandora* cycle of figures, which fuses the imagery of the [[motif:philosophers stone|philosophers' stone]] with explicitly Christian symbolism — the redeeming lapis, coats of arms of the work, and emblematic personifications of its stages.

## Significance
The *Pandora* is among the most influential German alchemical picture-books of the sixteenth century, drawing on the *Buch der heiligen Dreifaltigkeit* tradition. Its programme of equating the [[motif:christ in the lapis|stone with Christ]] made it a touchstone for the religious reading of alchemy and shaped later compilations.

## For artists & game designers
Devotional-alchemical hybrid imagery: heraldic shields and a redeemer-figure standing in for the prize substance.

citations.text: Hieronymus Reusner (comp.), *Pandora, das ist, Die edleste Gab Gottes* (Basel, 1582).

entries.title: *Pandora* (1582) — Emblematic Plate (second figure)
entries.creator: Hieronymus Reusner (compiler)
entries.summary:
A second emblematic figure from the *Pandora* of 1582, the noblest-gift-of-God picture-book of German alchemy.

## Iconography
Another of the *Pandora*'s woodcut emblems, presenting the figures and arms of the work in the book's characteristic blend of laboratory allegory and Christian symbol.

## Significance
The *Pandora* transmitted the *Buch der heiligen Dreifaltigkeit* imagery to a wide sixteenth-century readership, fixing the equation of the [[motif:philosophers stone|stone]] with sacred history. Its emblems were copied and adapted across the German alchemical corpus.

## For artists & game designers
A matched companion plate to the first *Pandora* figure — useful as a paired 'page spread' asset.

citations.text: Hieronymus Reusner (comp.), *Pandora, das ist, Die edleste Gab Gottes* (Basel, 1582).

entries.title: *Pandora* (1582) — Emblematic Plate (later figure)
entries.creator: Hieronymus Reusner (compiler)
entries.summary:
A later emblematic figure from the *Pandora* (Basel, 1582), continuing the book's sequence of alchemical-Christian woodcuts.

## Iconography
The plate presents one of the cycle's later figures, with the arms and emblematic personages that key the stages of the work to its devotional programme.

## Significance
The completeness of the *Pandora*'s figure-cycle made it a visual encyclopedia of sixteenth-century German alchemy. Its imagery — the redeeming [[motif:philosophers stone|lapis]], the arms of the art, the [[motif:coniunctio|conjunction]] of principles — was repeatedly mined by later compilers and helped standardise the iconography of the [[motif:christ in the lapis|Christ-stone]].

## For artists & game designers
Late-sequence plate showing 'the work near completion' — pairs with the earlier figures as a progression set.

citations.text: Hieronymus Reusner (comp.), *Pandora, das ist, Die edleste Gab Gottes* (Basel, 1582).

entries.title: *Pretiosissimum Donum Dei* — Flask Emblem
entries.creator: Anonymous, after Georgius Aurach
entries.summary:
A flask emblem from the *Pretiosissimum Donum Dei* ("Most Precious Gift of God") tradition of Georgius Aurach, the sealed-vessel sequence that diagrams the colour-stages of the work.

## Iconography
A round philosophical flask holds the matter at one moment of its transformation; in the full series each vessel shows a successive colour — black, white, peacock, red — of the king-and-queen sealed within the [[motif:philosophical egg|philosophical egg]].

## Significance
The [[motif:flask sequence|Donum Dei]] presents the entire [[motif:philosophers stone|opus]] as a strip of sealed flasks, a format that became one of the standard ways of charting the colour-changes of the [[motif:coniunctio|chemical wedding]]. Its imagery feeds directly into the *Rosarium*, the *Splendor Solis* and the later rebis tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A flask-by-flask progress bar — each vessel a save-state of the transmutation's colour stage.

entries.title: *Aureum Vellus* — Title Page (Trismosin)
entries.creator: Salomon Trismosin
entries.summary:
The title page of the *Aureum Vellus* ("Golden Fleece"), the collection of alchemical treatises attributed to Salomon Trismosin that also transmitted the celebrated *Splendor Solis*.

## Iconography
The title-block announces the *Aureum Vellus oder Güldin Schatz und Kunstkammer*, framing the work under the myth of the [[motif:golden fleece|Golden Fleece]] of Jason — a standard alchemical allegory for the [[motif:philosophers stone|philosophers' stone]] won at the end of a heroic quest.

## Significance
The *Aureum Vellus* (Rorschach, 1598–1604) gathered Trismosin's treatises into a 'treasury and art-chamber' of the art, and is the textual home of the *Splendor Solis* miniatures. Its Golden-Fleece conceit became one of the most durable myths of alchemy, casting the adept as an Argonaut seeking the incorruptible gold.

## For artists & game designers
A quest-title screen: the Golden Fleece as the ultimate treasure-reward, with the adept as hero-questor.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin, *Aureum Vellus oder Güldin Schatz und Kunstkammer* (Rorschach, 1598–1604).

entries.title: *Aureum Vellus / Splendor Solis* — Emblematic Plate
entries.creator: Anonymous, after Salomon Trismosin
entries.summary:
An emblematic plate (image 8) from the *Aureum Vellus / Splendor Solis* of Salomon Trismosin.

## Iconography
The figure belongs to the Trismosin programme that culminates in the *Splendor Solis* cycle, presenting one stage of the work within the Golden-Fleece framing of the *Aureum Vellus*.

## Significance
The *Aureum Vellus* carried the [[motif:splendor solis|Splendor Solis]] imagery — among the most lavishly painted in all alchemy — to its early readers under the sign of the [[motif:golden fleece|Golden Fleece]]. The plates trace the [[motif:philosophers stone|opus]] from base matter to solar perfection in a sequence of richly bordered scenes.

## For artists & game designers
A bordered tableau-card in the Splendor Solis manner — ornate frame around a single transmutation scene.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin, *Aureum Vellus / Splendor Solis* (16th c.).

entries.title: *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678) — Title Page
entries.creator: Engraving for the *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum* (Frankfurt)
entries.summary:
The engraved title page of the *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt, 1678), the great anthology of alchemical treatises.

## Iconography
The title-block presents the *Musaeum Hermeticum* as a 'museum' or treasury of the art, framing the collection's promise to gather the most useful tracts of the [[motif:philosophers stone|stone]] under one cover, with emblematic figures attending the title.

## Significance
The *Musaeum Hermeticum* anthologised the central texts of German alchemy — the *Twelve Keys*, the *Tractatus Aureus*, the *Golden Tract*, the *Janitor Pansophus* and more — and its Merian-workshop engravings became the canonical illustrations of the late-seventeenth-century art. It is one of the most important printed compendia in the whole tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A library/codex title-screen: the 'museum of secrets' as a hub from which many treatises (quests) branch.

entries.title: *Musaeum Hermeticum* — Emblem (A050)
entries.creator: Matthäus Merian workshop (engraver)
entries.summary:
An emblem (A050) from the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678), one of the figured plates illustrating the treatises in the anthology.

## Iconography
The single emblem pairs a figurative alchemical scene with the visual conventions of the Frankfurt emblem-style — sun and moon, the matter in its vessel, the union of principles — in the crisp engraved manner of the Merian workshop.

## Significance
The figured emblems of the *Musaeum Hermeticum* distilled the [[motif:philosophers stone|opus]] into legible plates that travelled with the anthology across Europe. Such images formed the shared visual vocabulary of late-seventeenth-century [[motif:alchemy|alchemy]] and were copied for generations.

## For artists & game designers
Drop-in single-emblem art for an alchemy treatise page — clean engraving with classic motifs.

entries.title: Barchusen, *Elementa Chemiae* (1718) — The Laboratory
entries.creator: Johann Conrad Barchusen
entries.summary:
A laboratory plate from Johann Conrad Barchusen's *Elementa Chemiae* (Leiden, 1718), source of the famous series of small process-emblems.

## Iconography
The engraving shows the alchemical laboratory and its apparatus — furnaces, flasks and vessels — in the spare, practical style of an early-eighteenth-century chemistry textbook, the setting for Barchusen's run of numbered process-emblems.

## Significance
Barchusen's *Elementa Chemiae* sits at the threshold between emblematic [[motif:alchemy|alchemy]] and modern chemistry: its 78 small emblems compress the operations of the [[motif:philosophers stone|opus]] into a comic-strip-like sequence that was later read (by Jung and others) as a complete pictorial summary of the art. The work helped carry alchemical imagery into the Enlightenment laboratory.

## For artists & game designers
A crafting-bench backdrop and a ready 78-step process grid — each tiny emblem one recipe node.

entries.title: *Hermaphroditisches Sonn- und Mondskind* (1752) — Coloured Plate
entries.creator: Johann Augustin Brunnhofer (attrib.)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Sander Petrus). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A hand-coloured plate from the *Hermaphroditisches Sonn- und Mondskind* ("Hermaphroditic Sun-and-Moon Child," Mainz, 1752), attributed to Johann Augustin Brunnhofer.

## Iconography
The plate figures the [[motif:rebis hermaphrodite|sun-and-moon child]] — the offspring of the solar king and lunar queen — in vivid period colour, surrounded by the emblematic apparatus of the work.

## Significance
The *Sonn- und Mondskind* is one of the last great coloured alchemical emblem-books, its figures dramatising the [[motif:coniunctio|conjunction]] of solar and lunar principles and the birth of the [[motif:philosophers stone|stone]] as a divine child. Such mid-eighteenth-century colour-plate books mark the final flowering of the visionary German alchemical tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Lush full-colour rebis art — a 'birth of the prize' reveal screen in saturated period palette.

entries.title: *Janitor Pansophus* — Synoptic Emblem (variant)
entries.creator: Anonymous (engraver)
entries.summary:
A further plate in the manner of the *Janitor Pansophus* ("all-wise doorkeeper"), preserved in the Deutsche Fotothek theosophy-and-alchemy series.

## Iconography
A dense synoptic emblem gathers sun and moon, the planets, the beasts and vessels of the work around a central axis, presenting the whole of alchemical wisdom as a threshold or gateway to universal knowledge.

## Significance
The [[motif:janitor pansophus|Janitor Pansophus]], famously appended to the *Musaeum Hermeticum*, is a summary-emblem that tries to show all the correspondences of [[motif:macrocosm-microcosm|macrocosm and microcosm]] at once. Such synthesis-plates served as memory theatres of the art, a single image standing for an entire compendium.

## For artists & game designers
A 'map of everything' diagram — every game system encoded as one symbolic gateway screen.

citations.text: *Janitor Pansophus* emblem (Deutsche Fotothek theosophy / alchemy series).

entries.title: The Ideal Chemical House (*Domus Chymici*), Libavius *Alchymia* (1606)
entries.creator: Andreas Libavius
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The famous *Domus Chymici* — the plan and elevation of the ideal alchemical house — from Andreas Libavius's *Alchymia* (D.O.M.A., Frankfurt, 1606).

## Iconography
The plate lays out a complete purpose-built chemical house: rooms for the furnaces, the laboratory proper, storerooms, a chemists' chamber and even social spaces, drawn as an architectural plan with elevation. Every apparatus and operation is given its designated place.

## Significance
Libavius's *Alchymia* is often called the first systematic chemistry textbook, and the [[motif:ideal laboratory|Domus Chymici]] is its most celebrated image: a rationalised, almost utopian design for a building dedicated entirely to chemical work. It expresses the period's drive to bring [[motif:alchemy|alchemy]] into ordered, teachable, institutional form, and is endlessly reproduced in histories of chemistry.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made floor plan for an alchemist's headquarters — drop each room in as a craftable workshop tier.

entries.title: Alembics from Libavius, *Alchymia* (1606)
entries.creator: Andreas Libavius
entries.summary:
A plate of alembics and distillation glassware from Andreas Libavius's *Alchymia* (Frankfurt, 1606).

## Iconography
The engraving sets out a catalogue of alembics, retorts and receivers — the vessels of distillation — drawn in clear technical profile so each instrument can be identified and built.

## Significance
Libavius's *Alchymia* systematised the [[motif:apparatus|apparatus]] as much as the theory of the art; its plates of [[motif:alembics|alembics]] and furnaces gave chemists a standardised vocabulary of equipment. Together with the *Domus Chymici*, these instrument-plates mark the transformation of alchemy into a teachable laboratory discipline.

## For artists & game designers
An instrument reference sheet — drop-in concept art for craftable distillation gear.

entries.title: Title Page of Libavius, *Alchymia* (D.O.M.A., 1606)
entries.creator: Andreas Libavius
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The title page of Andreas Libavius's *Alchymia* (D.O.M.A., Frankfurt, 1606), one of the foundational printed works of systematic chemistry.

## Iconography
The title-block presents the work under the dedicatory formula *Deo Optimo Maximo Auspice* (D.O.M.A.), framing the comprehensive treatise on the operations and apparatus of [[motif:alchemy|alchemy]] that follows.

## Significance
Libavius's *Alchymia* is widely regarded as the first systematic textbook of chemistry, organising the whole field — substances, operations, instruments, the [[motif:ideal laboratory|ideal laboratory]] — into teachable order. Its appearance marks a decisive step in the disciplining of the art, even as Libavius retained the goal of the [[motif:philosophers stone|stone]].

## For artists & game designers
An authoritative tome cover — the 'master manual' item that unlocks the chemistry tech-tree.

entries.title: Glauber, *Furni Novi Philosophici* — Furnace Plate I
entries.creator: Johann Rudolph Glauber
entries.summary:
A furnace engraving from Johann Rudolph Glauber's *Furni Novi Philosophici* ("New Philosophical Furnaces," Amsterdam, 1648–50), his celebrated description of a new distillatory art.

## Iconography
The plate shows one of Glauber's new-design furnaces in technical section, with its retorts, receivers and draught arrangement laid out so the apparatus can be reproduced for distilling spirits, oils and medicines.

## Significance
Glauber's *Furni Novi Philosophici* re-engineered the [[motif:distillation furnace|distillation furnace]] for greater efficiency and reproducibility, and the work was a landmark of seventeenth-century practical chemistry. Glauber — discoverer of 'Glauber's salt' (sodium sulfate) — stands at the productive frontier where [[motif:alchemy|alchemy]] became chemical technology.

## For artists & game designers
A buildable furnace blueprint — a clean cross-section ideal for a crafting-station upgrade.

entries.title: Glauber, *Furni Novi Philosophici* — Furnace Plate II
entries.creator: Johann Rudolph Glauber
entries.summary:
A second furnace engraving from Glauber's *Furni Novi Philosophici* (Amsterdam, 1648–50).

## Iconography
The plate details another of Glauber's furnace designs and its glassware, drawn in working section for the distillation of spirits, oils and other preparations.

## Significance
The *Furni Novi Philosophici* presented a graded series of [[motif:distillation furnace|furnaces]], each tuned to a different class of operation — the practical core of Glauber's chemistry. The book's clear apparatus-plates made advanced distillation reproducible and influenced laboratory practice across Europe, marking the maturing of [[motif:alchemy|alchemy]] into chemical industry.

## For artists & game designers
A second furnace tier — pairs with plate I as an upgrade path in a crafting tree.

entries.title: Glauber, *Furni Novi Philosophici* — Furnace Plate III
entries.creator: Johann Rudolph Glauber
entries.summary:
A third furnace engraving from Glauber's *Furni Novi Philosophici* (Amsterdam, 1648–50), part of the apparatus series of the new philosophical furnaces.

## Iconography
The plate shows a further furnace-and-glassware arrangement in technical section, completing the trio of designs for distilling spirits, oils, flowers and medicines.

## Significance
Taken together, Glauber's furnace-plates form a practical manual of distillation that helped define seventeenth-century laboratory chemistry. The reproducibility they enabled is emblematic of the shift from secret [[motif:alchemy|alchemy]] toward an open, instrument-based science.

## For artists & game designers
A third furnace variant — a set of three reads as a tech progression for a distillation workshop.

entries.title: Glauber — Distillation Apparatus (*The Works*)
entries.creator: Johann Rudolph Glauber
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A distillation-apparatus plate from the English translation of Johann Rudolph Glauber's *Works* (London, 1689), in the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The engraving lays out furnaces, retorts and receivers in working arrangement, continuing the apparatus tradition of Glauber's *Furni Novi Philosophici* for an English readership.

## Significance
The translation of Glauber's *Works* carried his furnace-and-distillation designs into the Anglophone world, where they fed the experimental chemistry of the early Royal Society period. The plate documents how a continental alchemist-chemist's [[motif:apparatus|apparatus]] became part of the shared technical heritage of European [[motif:alchemy|chemistry]].

## For artists & game designers
An alternate-engraving-style apparatus sheet — useful as a 'translated foreign manual' variant asset.

citations.text: Johann Rudolph Glauber, *The Works of the Highly Experienced... Glauber* (London, 1689); Wellcome M0016548.
